Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source array using a GitHub archive URL constructed from variables `_pkgname` and `pkgver`, which is a common and safe practice. The archive is fetched via HTTPS from a known upstream repository (`https://github.com/xelerance/Openswan`). Checksums (md5, sha256, sha512) are provided for the source tarball, ensuring integrity.

Top-level code consists only of variable assignments and array definitions. The function `pkgver()` is not defined, so no dynamic version logic executes during sourcing. The use of `set -u` and `set +u` is for shell safety and does not introduce risk. There are no command substitutions, evals, or external downloads executed at parse time. All operations that modify the environment or run commands are safely confined within `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` — functions that are not executed under `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`.

Thus, running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` is safe: it will only fetch the declared source and parse the file without executing any harmful code.
</details>
<summary>Safe sourcing and fetching with trusted upstream</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe sourcing and fetching with trusted upstream[/]

LLM auditresponse for openswan.service:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `openswan.service` file is a standard systemd service unit used to manage the Openswan IPsec daemon. It declares proper dependencies (`After=network.target`), uses `Type=forking` appropriate for daemons that fork after start, and sets up runtime directories with secure permissions (`RuntimeDirectoryMode=0700`). The `ExecStart`, `ExecStop`, and `ExecReload` directives point to a local script `/usr/lib/systemd/scripts/ipsec` with arguments to control the service. This pattern is consistent with safe, conventional service management.

The service script path `/usr/lib/systemd/scripts/ipsec` is within a system directory, but there is no evidence here of malicious behavior. Such scripts are typically bundled with the package and auditable as part of it. No network calls, dynamic payload execution, or obfuscated commands are present in the service file itself. The `Restart=always` directive is common for critical network services and not suspicious.

This file aligns with standard packaging practices for system daemons and shows no signs of a supply-chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
Standard systemd service file; no malicious behavior detected.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed openswan.service. Status: SAFE -- Standard systemd service file; no malicious behavior detected.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD file for <code>openswan</code> follows standard AUR packaging practices and shows no signs of malicious behavior. It downloads the source from the project's official GitHub repository (<code>https://github.com/xelerance/Openswan</code>) using a tagged release archive, which is a common and acceptable method for VCS-based packages. The checksums (md5, sha256, sha512) are provided and consistent with the source, indicating attention to integrity verification.

The build process applies harmless sed patches to adapt upstream build scripts for Arch Linux conventions (e.g., correcting install paths, disabling overly strict compiler flags, fixing tool calls like <code>egrep</code>). These are typical system-specific adjustments. The <code>build()</code> function uses symlinks to <code>/usr/bin/false</code> to stub out optional tools like <code>hostname</code> and <code>xmlto</code>, preventing unwanted behavior during build — a safe and common technique.

No network requests, code execution from external sources, data exfiltration, or suspicious file operations are present. All modifications are transparent and serve the purpose of integrating the upstream software into Arch Linux properly. The package installs systemd service files, adjusts permissions, and handles standard directories appropriately.

While the use of <code>ln -sf '/usr/bin/false'</code> may look unusual at first glance, it is a known method to prevent certain tools from being invoked during builds when they are not needed, and it does not introduce security risks.
</details>
<summary>
Standard Arch Linux packaging; no malicious content found</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for buildwin.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `buildwin.sh` script contains a single command: `make OS=cygwin BUILDENV=cygwin32-linux programs`. This command invokes the `make` build system with environment variables set to target a Cygwin-based build environment. The script does not perform any network operations, file deletions, obfuscated code execution, or system modifications outside the expected scope of a build script.

There are no uses of dangerous functions like `eval`, `curl`, `wget`, or `base64` decoding. The script only calls `make` with arguments appropriate for cross-compiling OpenSwan for a Windows environment using Cygwin, which aligns with the package's intended functionality. No data exfiltration, unexpected downloads, or suspicious operations are present.

This script is consistent with standard build practices for the OpenSwan project and shows no signs of tampering or malicious code injection.
</details>
<summary>
Standard build script, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for openswan-2.4.0-SecureClient.diff:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided diff file adds support for CheckPoint SecureClient (CPSC) authentication to the OpenSwan IPsec implementation. It introduces new attribute definitions, state machine transitions, policy flags, and conditional logic to handle CPSC-specific IKE payloads and authentication flows, building upon the existing XAUTH framework.

No malicious or suspicious behavior is present. The changes are confined to implementing a legitimate VPN client authentication protocol (CheckPoint SecureClient). The code does not introduce network exfiltration, unauthorized file access, code execution from external sources, or obfuscated payloads. All modifications are structural and functional, consistent with extending the supported authentication methods of the IPsec daemon. The use of `DBG_PRIVATE` on a different bit (21 instead of 20) is benign and does not imply data leakage.

The inclusion of password and challenge-handling logic is appropriate for a security client feature and occurs within the established XAUTH codepath, with no evidence of logging, transmission, or storage of credentials outside the authentication context. The feature is conditionally compiled and disabled by default (`default("cpsc", "no")`), which aligns with secure design principles.
</details>
<summary>
Adds CheckPoint SecureClient support; no malicious code detected
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for buildlin.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `buildlin.sh` is a simple shell script designed to check for the presence of required build dependencies (gmp, bison, flex, make) before invoking `make` to build the openswan project. It outputs helpful instructions for installing missing dependencies on Debian or Red Hat-based systems. All operations are transparent and use standard Unix tools without obfuscation.

The script checks common include paths for `gmp.h`, verifies essential build tools, and exits early if any are missing. The use of `make USE_OBJDIR=true programs` is consistent with the project's build process and does not introduce external downloads, eval calls, or data exfiltration. Setting `WERROR` triggers only a warning delay, which is benign.

There are no suspicious network operations, file modifications outside the build context, or execution of fetched payloads. The script serves a clear, expected role in the build environment validation and poses no supply-chain risk.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious behavior detected; standard build script.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for Makefile:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Makefile is part of the OpenSwan IPsec implementation and contains standard build logic for compiling and installing kernel modules and user-space programs. It includes rules for patching kernel source code, building modules out-of-tree, and installing binaries — all consistent with the expected functionality of a kernel module packaging system.

The file does not contain any suspicious network operations, obfuscated code, or unauthorized file manipulations. All operations are directed toward building and installing the OpenSwan software, with explicit paths to kernel sources and use of standard build tools (make, patch, cp, mv, etc.). The scripts invoked (e.g., `packaging/utils/kernelpatch`, `makeshadowdir`) appear to be local helper scripts within the source tree and are used for legitimate build customization.

There is no evidence of data exfiltration, remote code execution, or backdoor installation. The use of `patch` is limited to applying IPsec-related kernel patches from local files, and there are no calls to download content from external sources. The makefile respects standard conventions for kernel module compilation and installation, including proper use of `$(KERNELSRC)` and `$(MODLIB)`.

While it modifies kernel source temporarily via patching, this is a documented and expected behavior for OpenSwan's KLIPS module, not a supply-chain attack. The code aligns with the project's historical design and presents no signs of tampering.
</details>
<summary>
Standard build system with no malicious behavior detected
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for 10-initd-header.patch:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a patch for the OpenSwan IPsec service init script, specifically modifying the `Default-Start` runlevels in `setup.in` from being empty to explicitly starting in runlevels 2, 3, 4, and 5. This is a standard modification for ensuring the IPsec service starts correctly during boot on SysVinit systems.

There are no signs of malicious activity. The change is transparent, minimal, and consistent with legitimate service packaging requirements. It does not introduce obfuscated code, network exfiltration, unexpected downloads, or modifications outside the scope of service initialization. The patch applies solely to configuration within the package's own build source.

The patch author is credited, and the modification aligns with standard system administration practices for service enablement. No dangerous commands or suspicious constructs (e.g., `curl|bash`, `eval`, `base64` decoding) are present.
</details>
<summary>
Benign init script modification for service enablement</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for BASUninstall.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `BASUninstall.sh` is designed to remove specific files and unload a launch daemon associated with the Openswan service on macOS systems. It uses standard system commands (`sudo launchctl unload`, `sudo rm`) to remove a plist file, a privileged helper tool, and a Unix socket. All paths referenced are standard locations for macOS system services and daemons, and the operations are consistent with uninstalling a system service.

There is no evidence of malicious behavior such as downloading remote content, exfiltrating data, obfuscation, or execution of unauthorized commands. The script only removes files it would have installed as part of a corresponding install process, which is typical for uninstallation scripts provided by legitimate software vendors. The copyright and disclaimer belong to Apple Inc., and while this script may have been adapted from an Apple sample, its current use appears appropriate and non-malicious.

The presence of `sudo` indicates elevated privileges are required, but this is expected when modifying system-level services. No unexpected network activity, shell injection, or suspicious file operations are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard uninstall script; no malicious behavior detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for oid.pl:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `oid.pl` script is a Perl utility designed to generate C header (`oid.h`) and source (`oid.c`) files from a text file (`oid.txt`) containing object identifier (OID) definitions. It performs no network operations, does not execute external commands, and does not access sensitive system files. The script reads `oid.txt`, parses its contents to extract OID values and names, and writes structured C data to files in the expected locations for use by the OpenSwan IPsec implementation.

All file operations are confined to writing within the package's build tree (`../../include/oid.h` and `oid.c`), which is standard behavior for a code generation script in a build system. The script includes appropriate error checking when opening files and validates input line format, reducing the risk of malformed input causing unexpected behavior. There are no signs of obfuscation, data exfiltration, dynamic code evaluation (e.g., `eval`), or execution of shell commands.

The copyright notices, comments, and automatic generation warnings are benign and typical for such auto-generated code. The script's logic is transparent and narrowly focused on converting textual OID data into a C data structure, consistent with the stated purpose.
</details>
<summary>
Code generator with no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for pcf2os.pl:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Perl script `pcf2os.pl` is a configuration conversion tool that transforms Cisco VPN client `.pcf` files into OpenSwan-compatible `ipsec.conf` and `ipsec.secrets` configurations. It reads the input `.pcf` file, extracts values such as `description`, `host`, `groupname`, and `grouppwd`, and outputs them in the appropriate OpenSwan configuration format. The script performs no network operations, executes no external commands, and does not use dangerous functions like `eval`, `system`, or `open` with user-controlled arguments beyond the input file. All operations are limited to parsing and printing configuration stanzas.

The script does not exfiltrate data, download remote content, or modify system files. It only prints configuration output to stdout, which must be redirected manually by the user to create actual configuration files. While it handles credentials (group password) in plaintext, this is inherent to the conversion task and not a supply-chain issue. No obfuscation, suspicious encoding, or unusual behavior is present.

This script aligns with its stated purpose and poses no evidence of malicious intent or supply-chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
Configuration converter; no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for upd.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `upd.sh` contains a single command that executes an SSH connection to a remote server (`credil@galaxy.gatineau.credil.org`) and runs a script `bin/update-archive.sh` on that server. This represents a significant security risk because:

- The script triggers an unguarded SSH command that could perform arbitrary actions on a remote host under the `credil` account.
- It may be used to execute remote operations with no audit trail or local validation, potentially enabling exfiltration, privilege escalation, or persistence on the remote system.
- While SSH itself is not inherently malicious, automatically connecting and executing a remote script without user interaction or clear context is atypical for a standard AUR packaging workflow and suggests a supply-chain risk if this file were part of a public AUR package.

This does not align with standard AUR packaging practices, which typically involve building and packaging from upstream sources. Instead, it appears to be a maintainer-specific automation tool that, if distributed in a package, could be abused or inspected for credentials.
</details>
<summary>
Remote SSH execution without safeguards</summary>
</security_assessment>

[9/10] Reviewing src/Openswan-3.0.0/CROSSCOMPILE.sh...
x Reviewed src/Openswan-3.0.0/packaging/debian/upd.sh. Status: UNSAFE -- Remote SSH execution without safeguards
LLM auditresponse for CROSSCOMPILE.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script CROSSCOMPILE.sh is a basic shell script intended for cross-compiling the Openswan package for an ARM architecture. It sets environment variables such as `CC`, `LD`, `AR`, and others to point to cross-compilation tools in a specified directory (`/usr/local/arm_tools`). It also configures `CFLAGS` via `USERCOMPILE` to include architecture-specific options, debug symbols, and workarounds for compiler limitations.

There are no network operations, no downloads, no file exfiltration, and no use of dangerous patterns like `eval`, `curl`, or `base64` decoding. The script does not modify system files, install services, or inject code. It solely prepares the build environment for a manual `make` invocation, which is a normal part of cross-compilation workflows. The comments provide context about executable formats (e.g., elf2flt for FLAT/BLFT) but do not introduce malicious behavior.

The use of `DESTDIR=/tmp/openswan.arm` for staged installation is standard practice and poses no inherent security risk. The script does not execute anything automatically—it merely exports variables and requires the user to manually run `make`. All content aligns with expected developer workflow for embedded builds.
</details>
<summary>
Cross-compilation setup; no malicious activity</summary>
</security_assessment>

# Issues (1 total)

## upd.sh

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: Remote SSH execution without safeguards

Details:

The script `upd.sh` contains a single command that executes an SSH connection to a remote server (`credil@galaxy.gatineau.credil.org`) and runs a script `bin/update-archive.sh` on that server. This represents a significant security risk because:

- The script triggers an unguarded SSH command that could perform arbitrary actions on a remote host under the `credil` account.
- It may be used to execute remote operations with no audit trail or local validation, potentially enabling exfiltration, privilege escalation, or persistence on the remote system.
- While SSH itself is not inherently malicious, automatically connecting and executing a remote script without user interaction or clear context is atypical for a standard AUR packaging workflow and suggests a supply-chain risk if this file were part of a public AUR package.

This does not align with standard AUR packaging practices, which typically involve building and packaging from upstream sources. Instead, it appears to be a maintainer-specific automation tool that, if distributed in a package, could be abused or inspected for credentials.

---

- While SSH itself is not inherently malicious, automatically connecting and executing a remote script without user interaction or clear context is atypical for a standard AUR packaging workflow and suggests a supply-chain risk if this file were part of a public AUR package.

This does not align with standard AUR packaging practices, which typically involve building and packaging from upstream sources. Instead, it appears to be a maintainer-specific automation tool that, if distributed in a package, could be abused or inspected for credentials.

The PKGBUILD's global scope and source array do not contain any malicious code. No obfuscated commands, unexpected network requests, or dangerous top‑level operations (like `eval`, `curl`, `wget`, or command substitution) are present. Only the static global scope is sourced, and that is benign.
</details>

LLM auditresponse for des-586.pl:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a Perl script that generates x86 assembly code for DES encryption/decryption routines. It is part of the OpenSSL/OpenSWAN cryptographic library's assembly language build system. The script includes standard helper modules (x86asm.pl, cbc.pl, desboth.pl) and defines assembly subroutines for DES operations (des_encrypt, des_encrypt2, des_encrypt3, des_decrypt3, and CBC modes). All references are to standard cryptographic lookup tables (des_SPtrans). There are no network requests, obfuscated code, file exfiltration, or unexpected system modifications. The script performs only legitimate cryptographic code generation for the package's intended purpose.
</details>
<summary>Standard cryptographic assembly generator; no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for openswan-2.6.25-SecureClient.diff:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This diff adds support for CheckPoint SecureClient (CPSC) authentication, a proprietary extension to the IKEv1 XAUTH protocol. The changes are consistent with adding a new authentication method: new constants, state machine entries, configuration options, and modifications to attribute handling. There is no evidence of obfuscated code, network requests, data exfiltration, or backdoors.

**Security concern:** The patch unconditionally bypasses RSA signature verification in `ikev1_main.c` by replacing `RSA_check_signature` with `r = STF_OK;`. This is a significant weakening of authentication for the HybridInitRSA method used by CheckPoint. However, this is an intentional design choice for interoperability with CheckPoint's proprietary protocol, not a hidden supply-chain attack. The code is transparent and serves the stated purpose of supporting CheckPoint clients. No other malicious behavior is present.
</details>
<summary>Adds CheckPoint SecureClient support; signature verification bypass noted but not malicious.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for upd.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `upd.sh` contains a single command:
```sh
ssh credil@galaxy.gatineau.credil.org bin/update-archive.sh
```
This command connects to a remote host (`galaxy.gatineau.credil.org`) via SSH and executes a script (`bin/update-archive.sh`) on that host. The remote host is not an official upstream source for the `openswan` project (which typically uses domains like `openswan.org` or `xelerance.com`). This constitutes a supply-chain risk: if the remote server is compromised or under an attacker's control, the executed script could perform arbitrary malicious actions on the builder's machine (with the privileges of the SSH user `credil`). Even if this script is intended only for maintainer convenience, its inclusion in a distributable package raises the danger of accidental execution or misuse. This behavior aligns with the guidelines for marking UNSAFE: executing code from an unexpected host without validation.
</details>
<summary>SSH to remote host executes arbitrary script.</summary>
</security_assessment>

# Issues (1 total)

## upd.sh

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: SSH to remote host executes arbitrary script.

Details:

The file `upd.sh` contains a single command:

This command connects to a remote host (`galaxy.gatineau.credil.org`) via SSH and executes a script (`bin/update-archive.sh`) on that host. The remote host is not an official upstream source for the `openswan` project (which typically uses domains like `openswan.org` or `xelerance.com`). This constitutes a supply-chain risk: if the remote server is compromised or under an attacker's control, the executed script could perform arbitrary malicious actions on the builder's machine (with the privileges of the SSH user `credil`). Even if this script is intended only for maintainer convenience, its inclusion in a distributable package raises the danger of accidental execution or misuse. This behavior aligns with the guidelines for marking UNSAFE: executing code from an unexpected host without validation.
